aliases: Ensaimada (original Mallorcan version — different: uses saïm/lard, no cheese)
Ensaymada (pan de Manila, Filipino cheese bun, Filipino sweet bread)

romanized: Ensaimada (original Mallorcan version — different: uses saïm/lard, no cheese)
Ensaymada (pan de Manila, Filipino cheese bun, Filipino sweet bread)

region_of_origin: Philippines (Spanish colonial origin via Mallorca, Spain)
food_subgroup: Sweet enriched bread — brioche-style Filipino bun with butter, granulated sugar, and grated cheddar or Edam cheese topping; high glycaemic load per piece

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for ensaymada. Closest parent concept: enriched sweet wheat dough bread roll (FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Filipino ensaymada as of 2026).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for ensaymada. Closest parent category: sweet enriched bread / brioche-type roll. Using A0001 for 'Sweet enriched bread roll' as the closest available FoodEx2 code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ensaymada or Filipino sweet brioche bun as of 2026.

preparation_method: topped with grated cheddar or Edam cheese

serving.description: 1 piece ensaymada (~80g: bun ~60g + butter topping ~8g + sugar ~5g + cheese ~8g; Manila bakery standard)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Manila bakery ensaymada piece weighing approximately 80g, comprising a soft enriched wheat bun (~60g) topped with softened butter (~8g), granulated sugar (~5g), and grated cheddar or Edam cheese (~8g), providing approximately 276 kcal per piece.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Branded Foods and SR Legacy (closest match: enriched brioche-style sweet bread roll); supplemented with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Tables for Philippine bakery product estimates
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) + FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for Filipino ensaymada exists. Values derived from USDA FoodData Central closest match (enriched sweet brioche-style bread roll, USDA SR Legacy) combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA estimates for Philippine bakery products. Butter, sugar, and cheese topping contributions calculated from standard component weights (butter ~8g, sugar ~5g, cheddar/Edam ~8g per 80g piece). Per-100g values represent the composite bun-plus-topping product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Bread Products — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on enriched white wheat bread dough (GI ~70–75 for plain white bread; butter, egg, and sugar enrichment lowers effective GI to ~65 for brioche-type products)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Filipino ensaymada. GI estimated at 65 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for enriched white wheat bread and brioche-type rolls. Plain white bread GI ~75; fat and egg enrichment in brioche-type doughs reduces GI to approximately 65 per published analogous data. GL per 80g serving = (65 × 37.6g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.4 (HIGH GL). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~224mg per 80g piece, primarily from cheese topping)
hypertension.guidance: One ensaymada piece contributes approximately 224mg sodium — comparable to two slices of white bread — and is acceptable within a hypertension-managed diet when consumed occasionally; avoid pairing with other high-sodium items such as processed meats or salty beverages at the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load per piece (GL ~24) driven by refined white wheat flour and ~12g added sugar per 80g piece
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ensaymada carries a HIGH glycaemic load (~24 per piece) and should be treated as an occasional item rather than a daily breakfast for people with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed, pair with a protein source such as a boiled egg to moderate postprandial glucose response, and avoid co-consumption of sweetened condensed milk coffee (kopi susu) which can raise combined breakfast GL to ~38.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~6g per 80g piece from butter and cheese topping, plus trace trans fat (~0.16g per piece) from commercial shortening in industrial bakery versions
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each ensaymada piece provides approximately 6g saturated fat (30% of a 20g daily SFA limit) and trace trans fat; individuals with established cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional treats and choose artisan butter-based versions over industrial shortening-based versions to minimise trans fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat per piece; calcium contribution from cheese topping supports bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: At one piece per occasion, saturated fat is manageable for rheumatoid arthritis patients; the cheese topping contributes approximately 52mg calcium per piece, which is a positive contributor to bone health, though refined carbohydrate content may transiently elevate postprandial blood glucose and contribute to low-grade inflammation if consumed daily.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ensaymada is a Filipino sweet enriched brioche-style bread bun weighing approximately 80g per standard Manila bakery piece. It is made from an enriched wheat flour dough containing eggs, butter, milk, sugar, and yeast, baked until soft and fluffy, then topped generously with softened butter, granulated sugar, and grated cheddar or Edam (Gouda-style) cheese — a uniquely Filipino sweet-savoury flavour combination. The texture is pillowy, tender, and slightly chewy with a buttery, sweet, and mildly salty-savoury finish from the cheese. Ensaymada originated from the Spanish colonial period when the Mallorcan ensaimada (a coiled lard-based pastry) was introduced to the Philippines; Filipino bakers adapted it by substituting butter for lard, adding sugar topping, and introducing grated cheese — creating a distinctly Filipino product. Per 80g piece: approximately 276 kcal, 6.4g protein, 11.2g fat (6g saturated fat), 37.6g carbohydrate (12g added sugar), and 224mg sodium. Glycaemic index ~65 (medium), glycaemic load ~24 per piece (HIGH). Premium bakery versions (100–150g) can reach 380–560 kcal. Ensaymada is a staple of Filipino bakeries (panaderya), commonly consumed as breakfast or merienda (afternoon snack). In Singapore, it is available at Filipino bakeries and cafés in Lucky Plaza and other Filipino community hubs. Nutritionally, it is high in refined carbohydrates and saturated fat; it is not suitable as a daily breakfast for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GL. Source: USDA FoodData Central (adapted from enriched brioche-type bread roll) + FAO/INFOODS SEA. Confidence: HIGH.
meal_context: Filipino bakery breakfast Singapore Lucky Plaza

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central (enriched brioche-type sweet bread roll, SR Legacy) adapted with component-level calculations for butter, sugar, and cheese topping contributions. FAO/INFOODS SEA used as corroborating regional source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for enriched white wheat bread and brioche-type rolls with documented estimation basis. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated. Confidence: HIGH.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for the composite ensaymada product (bun + butter + cheese topping) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026; individual component fatty acid data could be assembled but would introduce estimation uncertainty beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite baked product with toppings is not reported in the adapted source. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — enriched wheat bread products contain no vitamin C per USDA FoodData Central; this is a naturally zero value for this food category. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~65) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for enriched white wheat bread and brioche-type rolls; no direct GI measurement for Filipino ensaymada has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. GL per 80g serving calculated as (65 × 37.6g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.4 (HIGH). Premium bakery versions (100–150g) will have proportionally higher calorie and GL values. The original Mallorcan ensaimada uses saïm (pork lard) and is not halal; the Filipino adaptation uses butter and is halal-suitable. Industrial bakery versions may use vegetable shortening containing trace trans fats (~0.2g/100g).
